body:
## Problem

Leftover 1.x wording and unrecorded 2.0 facts across the docs:

1. CLAUDE.md "Adding a New Command" step 3 says to update a command table in .github/copilot-instructions.md — that file deliberately has no table (line 40).
2. CLAUDE.md Configuration lists only the five PostToolUse hooks; .claude/settings.json also registers a Stop hook (stop-reminder.py).
3. The `.claude/skills/nitpicker -> ../../skills/nitpicker` symlink is undocumented anywhere, and copilot-instructions calls .claude/skills/ "internal only".
4. AGENTS.md:3 says "28+ commands" (30 public + 1 internal exist).
5. .claude/skills/skills/SKILL.md Routing Guide covers 29 of 30 commands — `help` has no routing line.
6. README.md:148-150 points 1.x aliases at "the table above", which has no alias column.
7. .claude-plugin/marketplace.json:3 top-level description still says "Hostile audit skills" (plural 1.x framing).
8. skills/nitpicker/scripts/fetch-pr-comments.py:105 User-Agent is "ivuorinen-skills/cr-implementer" (retired skill name).

## Evidence

Each quoted location verified against the working tree on this branch.

## Impact

Contributors follow stale instructions; agent routing misses one command; cosmetic 1.x residue in shipped strings.

## Fix

One doc sweep: fix CLAUDE.md step 3 and the hooks list, document the symlink, update the count, add the help routing line, point README aliases at SKILL.md ## Commands, reword marketplace description, rename the User-Agent.

## Resolution

All eight items fixed: CLAUDE.md step 3 reworded, Stop hook and nitpicker symlink documented, AGENTS.md count updated to 30+, help routing line added, README aliases point at SKILL.md tables, marketplace description reworded, User-Agent renamed to nitpicker-cr.
title: Post-reorg documentation drift (eight small inconsistencies)

body:
## Problem

(1) validate-skills.yml never runs the new pre-commit suite, so markdownlint/yamllint/gitleaks gate only on contributor machines. (2) pre-commit pins ruff v0.15.20 while CI floats `uv run --with ruff` — rule sets can drift. (3) If "Validate" becomes a required status check, the PR paths filter means a PR touching only unlisted files never reports and blocks merge.

## Evidence

Workflow and .pre-commit-config.yaml quoted by reviewer; all three are latent, not currently failing.

## Impact

Lint gaps merge silently; contributor/CI lint disagreement; potential stuck PRs.

## Fix

Add a `pre-commit run --all-files` CI step; pin ruff in CI or accept drift; add a no-op fallback workflow if the check becomes required.

## Resolution

pre-commit suite now runs in CI (verified green locally first). Ruff pin drift accepted: pre-commit pins, CI floats. Paths-filter fallback deferred until Validate becomes a required check.
title: CI hardening opportunities: pre-commit suite not run in CI, ruff version drift, paths-filter vs required-check footgun

body:
## Problem

`phys = locations[0].get("physicalLocation", {})` (process-sarif.py:140) returns `None` when the key is present with a null value, then `phys.get("artifactLocation")` raises `AttributeError`. `_parse_sarif` only catches `json.JSONDecodeError`/`OSError`, so the exception escapes `main` and the process exits with no output.

## Evidence

Reproduced: `{"runs":[{"results":[{"ruleId":"r","message":{"text":"m"},"locations":[{"physicalLocation":null}]}]}]}` -> `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'`, no JSON emitted. SARIF emitters legitimately null `physicalLocation` for non-local findings.

## Impact

A single null-location result from any scanner discards every finding from every input file in that security run — the `security` command silently produces nothing.

## Fix

`phys = locations[0].get("physicalLocation") or {}` (matching the `or {}` already used for `region` two lines down), and guard `locations[0]` being non-dict.

## Resolution

Guard non-dict location and use 'or {}' for physicalLocation (process-sarif.py). Regression test test_null_physical_location_does_not_crash.
title: process-sarif crashes on physicalLocation:null, discarding all findings

body:
## Problem

(a) `.pytest_cache/` is ignored only by pytest's own nested .gitignore, not the repo's. (b) `skills/nitpicker/__pycache__/check-audit-consistency.cpython-314.pyc` is bytecode of a deleted script, still on disk. (c) validate-skills.yml lines 60-89 run six checks explicitly and then re-run all six via pre-commit — double work, two forms to keep in sync. (d) All six .claude/settings.json hooks are uv-invoked; without uv they error non-blockingly and local validation silently disappears (CI is the real gate — see the required-status-checks finding). (e) help.md:16-19 lists `release-gate [th]` in a modifiers list, reading as if it were a modifier.

## Evidence

`git status --ignored`; on-disk artifact listed; workflow lines quoted; hook commands all start `uv run`.

## Impact

Cosmetic to latent; nothing user-visible today.

## Fix

Add `.pytest_cache/` to .gitignore; delete stray `__pycache__` dirs; collapse CI to pre-commit + pytest + index-check; accept fail-open hooks (documented) once CI is required; move release-gate out of the modifier list.

## Resolution

.pytest_cache added to .gitignore; stray __pycache__ deleted; help.md release-gate moved out of modifier list
title: hygiene advisories: gitignore gap, stale bytecode, CI duplicate gates, fail-open hooks, help.md wording

body:
## Problem

Five of six hooks (validate-skill-hook, validate-json-hook, check-version-sync-hook, ruff-hook, stop-reminder) plus scripts/check-version-sync.py, scripts/list-skills.py, and common.collect_skills() have no tests at all. check-version-sync.py and list-skills.py run all logic at module top level, making them untestable without restructuring.

## Evidence

tests/ contains no test file for any of these; `pyproject.toml` `[tool.coverage.run] include` lists only the four shipped skill tools, so the holes are invisible to coverage measurement. Untested nontrivial logic includes validate-skill-hook's command-file→parent-SKILL.md rerouting (line 33) and stop-reminder's porcelain rename parsing and unparenthesized `or`/`and` filter (lines 33-40).

## Impact

A regression in any hook silently disables the repo's enforcement surface (compounded by the hook-protocol finding: failures are already invisible); version-sync drift detection can break unnoticed.

## Fix

Wrap module-level logic in `main()` for check-version-sync.py and list-skills.py; add hook tests following tests/test_validate_audit_findings_hook.py's pattern (feed stdin JSON, assert exit code and stderr); extend coverage include to internal scripts and hooks.

## Resolution

check-version-sync.py & list-skills.py wrapped in main(); test_hooks.py + coverage include added; bump-version restructured and tested
title: Enforcement hooks and internal scripts have zero test coverage

body:
## Problem

The pyproject version parser accepts only double-quoted values, so a valid single-quoted TOML version is misread as missing.

## Evidence

`check-version-sync.py:37` `re.match(r'^version\s*=\s*"([^"]+)"', line)` and the same pattern in `bump-version.py:60`. For the valid `version = '1.8.0'`, `read_toml_version` finds no match and raises `KeyError("[project] version field not found")`, so the checker fails with a false alarm and the bumper aborts.

## Impact

Latent (repo uses double quotes), but an equally valid single-quoted version breaks the gate and the bump on a correct, in-sync file.

## Fix

Accept either quote style: `re.match(r'''^version\s*=\s*["']([^"']+)["']''', line)`.

## Resolution

TOML version regex accepts single or double quotes in both scripts; single-quoted in-sync test proves no false alarm.
title: TOML version regex matches only double-quoted strings

body:
## Problem

Invoked with no arguments, the batch validator globs only `skills/*/SKILL.md`, silently omitting the five internal skills under `.claude/skills/` that the hook treats as valid skill trees.

## Evidence

`validate-skill.py:203` `targets = sorted(repo_root.glob("skills/*/SKILL.md"))`; `validate-skill-hook.py:36` accepts `.claude/skills/` and five skills live there. CI and `/validate-skills` mitigate by invoking it a second time with an explicit `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md` glob (`validate-skills.yml:44-45`), but a single ad-hoc `uv run scripts/validate-skill.py` reports "all validated" while half are unchecked.

## Impact

A one-shot manual run gives false assurance; only the double-invocation callers are safe.

## Fix

In the no-args branch also glob `.claude/skills/*/SKILL.md`.

## Resolution

validate-skill no-args discovery now includes .claude/skills/*; validates 7 skills; test_no_args_includes_dot_claude_skills.
title: No-args run silently skips the .claude/skills/ internal skills

body:
## Problem

`.pre-commit-config.yaml:60-65` runs `conventional-commit-msg` at the commit-msg stage, which CI's `pre-commit run --all-files` never executes; no CI step lints PR titles or commits.

## Evidence

A contributor without local pre-commit installed can land non-conventional commits.

## Impact

release-please mis-versions releases from malformed types (the exact defect class /nitpicker commits hunts).

## Fix

Add a SHA-pinned PR-title lint step (e.g. amannn/action-semantic-pull-request) — squash-merge means the PR title is what matters.

## Resolution

SHA-pinned amannn/action-semantic-pull-request pr-title job added (v6.1.1, verified SHA)
title: conventional-commit discipline enforced only at the local commit-msg stage

body:
## Problem

The block-list branch (check-rules-anatomy.py:59) requires `stripped.startswith("  - ")` — exactly two-space indent. YAML permits any consistent indentation, so a 4-space or tab-indented `paths:` list leaves `fm["paths"]` unset.

## Evidence

Agent-verified: `paths:\n    - "does/not/exist/**"` (4-space) -> zero findings; a `paths:\n    - "../../etc"` traversal glob likewise bypasses the traversal_glob guard entirely.

## Impact

Fail-open: the traversal_glob, absolute_glob, empty_glob and stale_glob checks never run for validly-indented rule files, so malformed/dangerous globs pass unaudited.

## Fix

Match `- ` after any leading whitespace: `re.match(r"\s+- ", line)` rather than a fixed 2-space prefix.

## Resolution

List-item parse now accepts any indent (lstrip + '- '). Test test_paths_list_parsed_at_non_two_space_indent.
title: check-rules-anatomy paths parser only accepts 2-space indent (fail-open)

body:
## Problem

The prose summary of what `make check` runs is missing two of its steps, in
three places that all copy the same incomplete list.

## Evidence

`Makefile:25`: `check: validate validate-rules version-sync audit-consistency index-check lint format-check test pre-commit`.
The summaries omit `index-check` (regenerates INDEX.md, fails if stale) and
`pre-commit` (markdownlint/yamllint/gitleaks):

- `CLAUDE.md:12` "validate skill+commands + validate-rules + version sync + findings-store validate + ruff lint + ruff format check + pytest"
- `README.md:162` (same list)
- `.github/copilot-instructions.md:98` (same list)

## Impact

Low: the target does more than documented, not less, so nothing breaks — but a
reader who trusts the summary won't know a stale INDEX.md or a markdownlint
failure will fail `make check`, which is exactly the index-check finding a fresh
contributor would hit.

## Fix

Append "+ findings-index check + pre-commit suite" to the three summaries, or
reword to "runs the full validate/lint/format/test/pre-commit pipeline (see the
`check:` target in Makefile for the exact ordered list)".

## Resolution

Added findings-index + pre-commit to the make check summaries in CLAUDE.md, README.md, .github/copilot-instructions.md.
title: make check one-line summary omits the index-check and pre-commit steps

body:
## Problem

Three defensive branches added earlier this session (commit 0f6227a) have no test exercising them, so a regression that removes or breaks the guard would not fail the suite:

1. `check-version-sync.py:76` — the empty-`vals` ERROR branch (`if not vals: print("... no version entries found")`).
2. `validate-json-hook.py:46` — the `except OSError: return` fail-open branch.
3. `list-skills.py:27` — the `max(20, ...)` width clamp.

## Evidence

- `grep` of `tests/test_check_version_sync.py` for an empty-plugins case → none.
- `grep` of `tests/test_hooks.py` / `tests/test_validate*.py` for `OSError`/directory-json/unreadable → none.
- `tests/` has no `test_list_skills*.py` at all.
- Each branch is present in current code (confirmed by grep), so the code exists but no test drives it.

## Impact

Low: the branches are defensive and low-risk, but they were added specifically to prevent crashes (IndexError, hook traceback, textwrap ValueError). Untested, a future refactor can silently drop the guard and reintroduce the exact crash they fixed — and this run's own findings (`audit-427a9c26`, `audit-704e2f15`, `audit-25e8e5df`) were resolved as "fixed" without the tests the audit fix-strategy requires.

## Fix

Add a test per guard using the existing patterns: an empty-`plugins` marketplace.json case in `test_check_version_sync.py` asserting a clean non-crash ERROR/exit 1; a directory-named-`x.json` (or unreadable) case in `test_hooks.py` asserting `validate-json-hook` exits 0 with no output. The `list-skills.py` clamp is a dev-only cosmetic path (needs a ~96-char name fixture) — cover it or record it as accepted-untested with that rationale.

## Resolution

Added test_empty_plugins_array_reports_error_not_crash (test_check_version_sync.py) and test_validate_json_unreadable_path_fails_open (test_hooks.py) — full suite now 354 passed. The list-skills.py max(20,...) clamp is left accepted-untested: it is a dev-only cosmetic path (make list) that only triggers on a >=96-char skill/command name, and adding a fixture for an impossible name is not worth the test.
title: Defensive branches added this session ship without test coverage

body:
## Problem

`validate-rules.py:43` matches list items only via `line.startswith("  - ")`. A `paths:` block indented 4 spaces (valid YAML) never populates `fm["paths"]` as a list, so `paths` reads as absent (:102) and the glob-freshness / must-be-a-list / traversal checks are skipped.

## Evidence

Agent-traced: same fail-open class as the shipped check-rules-anatomy.py:59 bug. A 4-space-indented glob list passes with its globs unvalidated.

## Impact

Fail-open in the rules validator: malformed or stale globs in a validly-indented rule file are not caught.

## Fix

Match `^\s+- ` with `current_key` set, rather than a fixed 2-space prefix.

## Resolution

parse_rules_frontmatter list-item parse accepts any indent. Test test_paths_list_parsed_at_four_space_indent.
title: validate-rules paths list parser only accepts 2-space indent (fail-open)

body:
## Problem

`render_finding` writes `f"{key}: {value}"` with no newline escaping (findings.py:172-184) and `parse_frontmatter` is last-write-wins per key (111-117). A newline inside --area/--title injects arbitrary frontmatter lines that override earlier keys.

## Evidence

Reproduced by reviewer: `--area $'x\nseverity: critical'` on a --severity low finding produces frontmatter with both `severity: low` and an injected `severity: critical`; on re-parse the finding reads back as critical and still passes validate.

## Impact

Any field (severity, status, id, auditor) can be silently corrupted by multi-line input — easy for an agent to hit accidentally.

## Fix

In `render_finding`, raise FindingError when a scalar field value contains a newline (or normalize with `value.replace("\n", " ")`). Add a regression test.

## Resolution

render_finding rejects newlines in title and all frontmatter values with FindingError. Test added.
title: Frontmatter injection via newlines in findings.py field values corrupts severity/status

body:
## Problem

The code comment describes the CVSS `security-severity` score as the
"authoritative signal when present", but the resolution logic takes the most
severe of CVSS and the tool string, so a coarse tool label can override CVSS.

## Evidence

`process-sarif.py:72` comment: "CVSS security-severity score (authoritative signal when present)".
`process-sarif.py:95-96`: `return min(candidates, key=lambda s: _SEVERITY_RANK[s])`
takes the most-severe of the CVSS-derived level and the tool-string level.
Input `security-severity:"2.0"` (CVSS -> Low) with `properties.severity:"CRITICAL"`
yields `Critical`, not the CVSS-derived Low — CVSS was not authoritative.

## Impact

Advisory: the "most severe wins" behaviour (argued for in the :93-94 comment) is
defensible; only the ":72 authoritative" wording contradicts it. No functional bug.

## Fix

Reword the :72 comment to "CVSS security-severity score (one signal; most-severe
of CVSS and tool string wins, see below)", consistent with :93-94.

## Resolution

Reworded process-sarif.py:72 comment to 'most severe of CVSS and tool string wins', consistent with the resolution logic.
title: process-sarif comment calls CVSS 'authoritative' but a coarser tool string can override it

body:
## Problem

`process-sarif.py` traverses SARIF JSON assuming every node has the expected
type, with no guarding. Several fields that SARIF permits to be null or
free-form crash the run with an uncaught traceback, and because `main()` loops
all input files through one aggregation, a single bad node throws away output
for every file already parsed.

## Evidence

Reproduced (each exits 1 with a traceback, no findings emitted):

- `properties.severity` non-string -> `process-sarif.py:89` `tool_severity.upper()`:
  input `{"properties":{"severity":5}}` -> `AttributeError: 'int' object has no attribute 'upper'`.
- `security-severity` non-numeric (list/dict) -> `process-sarif.py:75`
  `float(security_severity)`; the `except` at line 84 only catches `ValueError`,
  so a list raises an uncaught `TypeError: float() argument must be ... not 'list'`.
- `tool: null` -> `process-sarif.py:109/126`: `run.get("tool", {})` returns `None`
  (key present-but-null, so the `{}` default never applies) ->
  `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'get'`.
- a `run` that is a string (`{"runs":["notadict"]}`) -> `process-sarif.py:126`
  `'str' object has no attribute 'get'`. Same class at rules (`:113`) and taxa (`:157`).

`properties` is explicitly free-form in the SARIF spec, and real scanners emit
null `tool`/coarse types, so these are conforming-enough inputs, not fuzz.

## Impact

The `/nitpicker security` command feeds scanner SARIF through this shipped tool.
One malformed result silently drops the whole consolidated security report --
the audit reports nothing and exits non-zero mid-pipeline. Fail-loud-but-total
data loss on the security surface.

## Fix

Type-guard untrusted nodes before use:

- line 84: broaden to `except (ValueError, TypeError):`.
- line 89: `_TOOL_SEVERITY_MAP.get(str(tool_severity).upper())`.
- lines 109/126/113/157: coerce with `or {}` after the `.get` (not as the default)
  and `if not isinstance(run, dict): continue` for list items, e.g.
  `tool = run.get("tool") or {}; driver = tool.get("driver") or {}`.
Add a regression test with each malformed node asserting a clean skip + exit 0.

## Resolution

Type-guarded SARIF traversal (except TypeError, str() coercion, isinstance skips for run/rule/result/taxon, present-but-null tool). 4 malformed-node repros now exit 0; regression tests added.
title: process-sarif.py aborts the entire SARIF batch on a single non-conforming JSON node

body:
## Problem

(a) `json.JSONDecodeError` is not in the caught tuple `(KeyError, IndexError, OSError)` (lines 52-58), and the base package.json read (line 36) is unguarded. (b) `read_toml_version` (lines 28-33) takes the first `^version = ` match anywhere in pyproject.toml, while bump-version.py updates only the `[project]` section.

## Evidence

(a) A corrupt plugin.json (`{ corrupt`) dies with a raw traceback and stops before checking remaining files. (b) Any future `[tool.*]` table with a `version` key above `[project]` makes bump and check disagree permanently.

## Impact

Traceback instead of a clean MISMATCH/ERROR report; latent bump/check divergence.

## Fix

Add `json.JSONDecodeError` to both handlers and wrap the base read; anchor the TOML search to the `[project]` block (reuse bump-version's section-aware scan).

## Resolution

check-version-sync catches JSONDecodeError and anchors the pyproject read to [project]
title: uncaught JSONDecodeError and section-blind TOML version read

body:
## Problem

The migrate CLI branch (findings.py:672-679) catches only `FindingError`, but `migrate_v1` starts with `src.read_text()`.

## Evidence

`findings.py migrate /nonexistent.md` → raw FileNotFoundError traceback instead of the clean `ERROR <src>: ...` other subcommands emit.

## Impact

Inconsistent, hostile UX in a consumer-facing tool.

## Fix

Catch `(FindingError, OSError, UnicodeDecodeError)` in the migrate loop.

## Resolution

migrate CLI now catches (FindingError, OSError, UnicodeDecodeError)
title: migrate emits a raw traceback on a missing source file

body:
## Problem

Twenty command files state "Run standalone, by the /nitpicker default audit flow, and by the release gate" (agent-hooks.md:106-108 even scripts a "When run as a release gate" mode), but release-gate.md's behavior is `findings.py list --status open` plus a pass/fail report, explicitly "never resolves, edits, or files findings", and SKILL.md forbids chaining commands.

## Evidence

`grep -l "by the release gate\|As a release gate" skills/nitpicker/commands/*.md` → 20 files; release-gate.md:21-22 quoted above. complexity.md is stdout-only, so the store-reading gate could never see its results.

## Impact

Two authoritative texts contradict; an agent may chain commands the router forbids, or expect gate behavior that cannot occur.

## Fix

Delete "and by the release gate" (and agent-hooks' gate clause) from the 20 files — or amend release-gate.md and SKILL.md's no-chaining rule if gate-driven invocation is intended. One side must change.

## Resolution

'by the release gate' claim removed from all command files; release-gate.md unchanged
title: 20 command files claim invocation 'by the release gate'; release-gate runs nothing

body:
## Problem

`_normalize_severity` (process-sarif.py:71-88) checks the tool severity string first and returns on any map hit, before ever consulting the CVSS `security-severity` score. A result carrying both loses the authoritative CVSS signal.

## Evidence

Agent-verified: result `level:"warning"`, `properties.severity:"WARNING"`, rule `security-severity:"9.8"` -> output severity `Medium` (expected `Critical`). semgrep commonly sets both fields.

## Impact

Critical vulnerabilities are bucketed as Medium in `by_severity` and sort order — a security tool hides its most severe findings.

## Fix

Consult the numeric `security-severity` CVSS score before the tool string, or take `max()` of the two.

## Resolution

_normalize_severity now collects CVSS and tool-string candidates and returns the most severe; SARIF-level only as fallback. Test test_cvss_score_not_buried_by_coarse_tool_severity.
title: process-sarif tool-severity string overrides CVSS, silently downgrading Criticals

body:
## Problem

An explicit-null `uri` becomes `None` in a finding dict and crashes the final sort, so the whole run aborts with a traceback and emits nothing even though every input file parsed.

## Evidence

`process-sarif.py:161` `uri = (phys.get("artifactLocation") or {}).get("uri", "")` returns `None` when the `uri` key is present but null. The sort at line ~253 `unique.sort(key=lambda f: (_SEVERITY_RANK.get(...), f["uri"], f["start_line"]))` then compares `str` and `None` at the same severity → `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'str' and 'NoneType'`.

## Impact

One malformed location field discards all consolidated findings; the security command silently produces no report.

## Fix

Coerce at extraction: `uri = (phys.get("artifactLocation") or {}).get("uri") or ""`, or sort on `f["uri"] or ""`.

## Resolution

null artifactLocation.uri coerced to '' so the sort no longer compares str to None; test_null_uri_sorts_without_crash.
title: A null artifactLocation.uri crashes the sort and produces zero output

body:
## Problem

`auditor` is concatenated into paths with no validation in `new_finding` (findings.py:222), `resolve_finding` (246), and `_flush_v1` (433); `migrate` derives it from a filename stem, also unchecked.

## Evidence

## Problem

body:
## Problem

The stdlib-only enforcement script (added this session) has three robustness
gaps:

1. **Uncaught crash on non-UTF-8 input.** The parse guard catches
   `(OSError, SyntaxError)` but `script.read_text(encoding="utf-8")` raises
   `UnicodeDecodeError` (a `ValueError` subclass, not `OSError`) on a non-UTF-8
   file — so the gate aborts with a traceback instead of using its own
   "cannot parse" reporting path.
2. **Dynamic imports escape.** Detection is static `ast.Import`/`ast.ImportFrom`
   only. `importlib.import_module("requests")` or `__import__("yaml")` shows only
   `importlib` (stdlib), so the check passes while the tool breaks on a consumer.
3. **Interpreter-version skew.** `sys.stdlib_module_names` reflects the
   interpreter running the check, not the `requires-python >=3.11` floor.

## Evidence

- `except (OSError, SyntaxError)` does not include `UnicodeDecodeError`;
  a shipped file with a Latin-1 byte reproduces the traceback (agent-confirmed).
- `_module_roots` walks only `ast.Import`/`ast.ImportFrom`; a string-literal
  `importlib.import_module(...)` is invisible to it.
- `stdlib = set(sys.stdlib_module_names)` — if CI runs 3.13, a module that became
  stdlib in 3.13 is accepted though a 3.11 consumer would `ModuleNotFoundError`.

## Impact

Gap 1 fails closed (crash blocks the commit) but ungracefully, and can block an
unrelated commit that happens to touch a file with an odd byte. Gaps 2 and 3
are true false-negatives: a shipped tool can pass the gate and still break on a
consumer machine — the exact failure this check exists to prevent.

## Fix

1. Add `UnicodeDecodeError` to the caught tuple (it already has the
   `cannot parse — {e}` path), or read with `errors="replace"`.
2. Flag string-literal `__import__(...)` / `importlib.import_module(...)` calls,
   and document the residual dynamic-import limitation in the module docstring.
3. Note that the allowlist is the running interpreter's stdlib; run the check
   under the minimum supported Python (or pin a 3.11 stdlib set) to match the floor.

## Resolution

check-stdlib-only.py now catches UnicodeDecodeError (reports 'cannot parse' instead of crashing), detects string-literal `__import__`/`importlib.import_module` dynamic imports, and documents the residual non-literal-dynamic-import + interpreter-version-skew limits. Added tests: dynamic-import + non-utf8. Closure: 8 tests pass; a dynamic import of 'requests' is now flagged.
title: check-stdlib-only.py: crash on non-UTF-8 input, dynamic-import blind spot, interpreter-version skew

## Problem

`audit.md` lives inside `skills/nitpicker/commands/`. Its step 2 references the sibling checklist as `commands/_audit-coverage.md`, and steps 3-4 reference specialist files as `commands/<command>.md`. Every other command file references the sibling `_conventions.md` bare (no `commands/` prefix), and `audit.md` itself uses bare `_audit-coverage.md` two lines later (line 47) and bare `_conventions.md` (lines 32, 49). The `commands/`-prefixed form is both internally inconsistent and a wrong relative path — from `commands/audit.md`, `commands/_audit-coverage.md` resolves to `commands/commands/_audit-coverage.md`.

## Evidence

- `skills/nitpicker/commands/audit.md:33`: "Read `commands/_audit-coverage.md`" — the only `commands/_`-prefixed sibling reference in the entire skill (`grep -rn 'commands/_' commands/*.md` returns this one line).
- Same file, line 47: "the states `_audit-coverage.md` defines" — bare, correct.
- `_conventions.md:30` references `_audit-coverage.md` bare; all 20+ command files reference `_conventions.md` bare (a11y.md:57, arch.md:47, ci.md:22, …).
- Steps 3-4 also use `commands/<command>.md` for specialist files, same prefix inconsistency.

## Impact

Low: the filenames are unambiguous so an agent still finds them, but the wrong relative path and the inconsistency with the rest of the skill are a correctness/convention defect in newly shipped instructions — and this is the reference an audit run follows to build its coverage task list.

## Fix

Drop the `commands/` prefix from all three sibling references in `audit.md`: `commands/_audit-coverage.md` → `_audit-coverage.md` (step 2), and both `commands/<command>.md` → `<command>.md` (steps 3-4), matching the bare-reference convention used everywhere else.

## Resolution

Dropped the commands/ prefix from all three sibling references in audit.md: 'commands/_audit-coverage.md' -> '_audit-coverage.md' (step 2) and both 'commands/<command>.md' -> '<command>.md' (steps 3-4), matching the bare-reference convention. validate-skill + markdownlint pass.
title: audit.md references sibling files with a commands/ prefix, inconsistent with the bare-reference convention and its own text

body:
## Problem

The hook resolves the edited file's path (following symlinks) but compares it to
an *un*resolved store root, so when the checkout lives under a symlinked path the
two diverge and the hook silently no-ops: no finding validation, no INDEX.md
regeneration. Every sibling hook compares against `REPO_ROOT.resolve()`; this one
is the lone exception.

## Evidence

`scripts/hooks/validate-audit-findings-hook.py:51` resolves:
`path = (raw if raw.is_absolute() else REPO_ROOT / raw).resolve()`, but
`should_check` (:29) and the `is_index` test (:54) compare against
`store_root(REPO_ROOT)` (:24-25), which never calls `.resolve()`.

Reproduced with `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR=/tmp/repo-link` (symlink to the repo) and an
invalid finding `docs/audit/findings/zzztest/open/BOGUS.md` (no frontmatter):

- via the symlinked project dir -> exit 0, invalid file accepted, index not regenerated.
- via the real path (control) -> exit 2, "BOGUS.md is not a valid finding file".
`grep .resolve()` confirms validate-skill/validate-json/ruff/check-version-sync
all use `REPO_ROOT.resolve()`; only this hook uses the unresolved root.

## Impact

Any checkout under a symlinked path (symlinked `~/Code`, macOS `/tmp`->`/private/tmp`,
worktree layouts) silently loses the safety net CLAUDE.md advertises -- malformed
manual edits to the findings store slip through and INDEX.md rots undetected.

## Fix

Resolve the root at both comparison sites, matching the sibling hooks:
in `should_check` use `root = store_root(repo_root).resolve()`, and at :54
`path.is_relative_to(store_root(REPO_ROOT).resolve())`.

## Resolution

should_check and is_index now compare against store_root(...).resolve(), matching sibling hooks. Symlinked-dir repro now blocks (exit 2); regression test added.
title: validate-audit-findings-hook silently skips validation under a symlinked project dir

## Problem

`process-sarif` aborts the entire aggregation when any single input file fails to parse, throwing away findings already parsed from valid files.

## Evidence

`process-sarif.py:210-211` calls `sys.exit(1)` inside `_parse_sarif`, which runs per file from the loop at line ~249. `process-sarif good.sarif broken.json` exits during the second file; the parsed `good.sarif` findings are never emitted.

## Impact

Aggregating N scanner outputs (the primary use case) is all-or-nothing; one bad file yields no report.

## Fix

Collect per-file errors, warn and skip the bad file, and exit non-zero only after emitting findings from the files that parsed — or document the fail-fast contract explicitly.

## Resolution

process-sarif main() catches per-file SystemExit, skips the bad file, still emits good findings, exits 1 if any failed; test_bad_file_skipped_good_findings_emitted.

body:
## Problem

Finding writes are not crash-atomic and are inconsistent between the two writers.

## Evidence

`new_finding` writes directly: `findings.py:303` `path.write_text(render_finding(...))` — a crash mid-write (or after the `--force` `resolved_sibling.unlink` at line 301) leaves a truncated file. `resolve_finding` uses tmp+replace (`findings.py:339-341`) but then does a separate cross-directory `path.unlink()` (`findings.py:343`); a crash between the `replace` and the `unlink` leaves the id in both `open/` and `resolved/`, which `_find` (`findings.py:312-313`) then rejects as "matches multiple files", making the finding unresolvable.

## Impact

A process kill at the wrong moment corrupts the store (truncated file, or an id that can no longer be resolved/referenced and trips validate's duplicate-id check).

## Fix

Write `new_finding` via `path.with_suffix('.tmp')` + `replace`, matching `resolve_finding`. For `resolve`, when `_find` sees both copies prefer the `resolved/` one (reconcile) rather than erroring.

## Resolution

new_finding writes via .tmp + replace; _find returns the resolved/ copy when both copies exist (self-heals crash between resolve's replace and unlink). Tests: test_new_finding_leaves_no_tmp_file, test_find_prefers_resolved_when_both_copies_exist.
title: Non-atomic writes can leave a partial or duplicate finding file on crash

body:
## Problem

`validate_file` (findings.py:371-373) tests `re.search(rf"^## {section}\s*$", body, re.MULTILINE)` on the raw body with no fence-stripping, while `_normalize_body`/`migrate_v1` do track fences. Required sections that appear only inside a ``` code block satisfy the check.

## Evidence

Confirmed by inspection of findings.py:371-373: the OPEN_SECTIONS loop searches the unstripped body. A finding whose `## Evidence`/`## Impact`/`## Fix` exist only inside a fenced block passes `validate`.

## Impact

Fail-open: the store's own required-section gate can be bypassed, admitting findings missing three of four mandatory sections. Low real-world likelihood (author must bury all sections in a fence) but it is a validator false-negative.

## Fix

Strip fenced regions from `body` before the section search, reusing the fence-tracking loop already in `_normalize_body`.

## Resolution

Added _strip_fenced(); validate_file checks required sections against fence-stripped body. Test test_validate_flags_required_sections_hidden_in_code_fence.
title: findings.py section validation not fence-aware (fail-open)

body:
## Problem

Both the checker and the bumper only read/write the first entry of marketplace.json's `plugins` array, so a second plugin can desync undetected and unfixed.

## Evidence

`check-version-sync.py:19` `lambda o: o["plugins"][0]["version"]` and `bump-version.py:20` `lambda o, v: o["plugins"][0].__setitem__("version", v)`. With `plugins[1]` at a stale version and everything else in sync, the checker prints "All versions in sync." (exit 0) and the bumper never touches `plugins[1]`.

## Impact

Latent today (single plugin), but a future second marketplace plugin would silently pass the version gate while desynced.

## Fix

Iterate every element of `plugins`: check each `plugins[i]["version"]` against base and mutate each on bump.

## Resolution

check-version-sync + bump-version now iterate every marketplace plugins[] entry; stale-second-plugin test proves detection.
title: Version sync only inspects marketplace.json plugins[0]

body:
## Problem

All five PostToolUse hooks extract the edited path with `data.get("file_path") or data.get("path")` (validate-skill-hook.py:23, validate-json-hook.py:22, check-version-sync-hook.py:35, ruff-hook.py:24, validate-audit-findings-hook.py:44). Claude Code delivers PostToolUse stdin as `{"tool_name": ..., "tool_input": {"file_path": ...}, "tool_response": ...}` — the path is nested, so `file_path` is always empty and every hook silently no-ops.

## Evidence

Reproduced: `printf '{"tool_name":"Write","tool_input":{"file_path":"<repo>/.hooktest-bad.json"}}' | REPO_ROOT=$PWD uv run --quiet scripts/hooks/validate-json-hook.py` → rc=0, no output, on a file containing `{broken`. The same file with a synthetic top-level `{"file_path": ...}` payload → "INVALID JSON" + rc=1. All tests in tests/test_validate_audit_findings_hook.py feed the top-level shape, so make check stays green.

## Impact

Every hook guarantee in CLAUDE.md (SKILL validation, JSON validation, version-sync warning, ruff autofix, findings validation + INDEX regeneration) is dead in real sessions. A malformed finding file written by an agent is never caught and INDEX.md never regenerates.

## Fix

In each hook (or a shared helper in scripts/hooks/): `ti = data.get("tool_input") or {}; file_path = ti.get("file_path") or data.get("file_path") or data.get("path") or ""`. Add one test per hook using the real nested payload shape.

## Resolution

All five hooks now read tool_input.file_path first (top-level file_path/path kept as fallback); nested-payload regression tests added.
title: PostToolUse hooks read top-level file_path; real payloads nest it under tool_input — all five hooks never fire

body:
## Problem

`_pattern_covers` returns True on its third clause `base.startswith(rel + "/")`, which fires when a `.gitignore` pattern names a file *inside* the store rather than an ancestor of it. `is_store_gitignored` then reports the entire store as ignored.

## Evidence

With `rel = "docs/audit/findings"` and a `.gitignore` line `docs/audit/findings/resolved.jsonl` (or `.../INDEX.md`), `base = "docs/audit/findings/resolved.jsonl"` and `base.startswith(rel + "/")` is True, so `is_store_gitignored()` returns True. Then `ensure_store_gitattributes()` (line 446) returns early and never writes `.gitattributes`, and `check_review_hygiene()` (line 458) returns None so `validate` prints no warning. The open finding files and INDEX.md end up neither gitignored nor marked `linguist-generated`.

## Impact

Gitignoring any single file inside the store silently disables the generated-artifact marking with no warning; audit runs then flood PR diffs — the exact outcome the feature exists to prevent.

## Fix

Drop the `or base.startswith(rel + "/")` clause. "Covers" means the pattern is `rel` itself or an ancestor directory of `rel`; a descendant pattern does not cover the store.

## Resolution
Dropped the descendant clause in _pattern_covers; a gitignore pattern inside the store no longer reads as the whole store being ignored. Test test_pattern_covers_ignores_descendant_of_store added.
title: _pattern_covers treats a gitignored descendant as the whole store being ignored, disabling review-hygiene

body:
## Problem

`parse_rules_frontmatter` only understands block-sequence lists (`- item`). A flow-style list `paths: ["src/**"]` is stored as the raw scalar string `["src/**"]`, then `validate` rejects it because it is not a `list`.

## Evidence

For `paths: ["src/**"]`, line 56 does `val = val.strip().strip("\"'")` which yields the string `["src/**"]` (brackets are not quotes), so `fm["paths"]` is a str; line 113 `if not isinstance(paths_val, list): err("'paths:' must be a list of glob strings, not a scalar")` then exits 1, blocking commit/CI. The shipped sibling `skills/nitpicker/scripts/check-rules-anatomy.py:71-74` accepts the same input, so the two validators return opposite verdicts on identical valid YAML.

## Impact

A legitimately formatted rule file (`paths: ["a", "b"]`) fails `make validate`/CI with a wrong error message. Latent today because all current rule files use block-style `paths:`.

## Fix

Add the flow-style branch already present in check-rules-anatomy.py: when `val.startswith("[") and val.endswith("]")`, split on commas and strip quotes into a list before assigning. (Consolidating the two parsers — see the arch finding — fixes this at the root.)

## Resolution
validate-rules parser now handles YAML flow-style paths lists, matching check-rules-anatomy. Test test_flow_style_paths_list_accepted added.
title: validate-rules rejects a valid flow-style paths list, diverging from the shipped anatomy checker

body:
## Problem

`resolve_finding` sets `resolved_at = date or _today()` with no format check on the user-supplied `--date`, so a malformed value is persisted into the ledger record's `resolved` field.

## Evidence

Line 550 `resolved_at = date or _today()`; the `--date` CLI argument (line 1052) has no validator. `resolve <id> --status fixed --notes n --date garbage` writes `"resolved": "garbage"` into `resolved.jsonl`. It is only caught afterward by `validate_ledger_record` (line 692), i.e. after the bad data is already committed to the append-only ledger.

## Impact

A typo'd date lands permanently in the append-only ledger; the store then validates dirty until the ledger is hand-edited, which the design forbids.

## Fix

Validate `date` against `_DATE` inside `resolve_finding` before writing and raise `FindingError` on mismatch (apply the same to any other externally supplied date path).

## Resolution
resolve_finding now validates --date against _DATE before writing to the ledger. Test test_resolve_rejects_malformed_date added.
title: resolve --date is written into the append-only ledger without format validation

body:
## Problem

`resolve_finding` reads the open file, appends to the ledger, then calls a bare `path.unlink()` with no `missing_ok` and no atomicity. If the open file is gone between the glob and the unlink (a second resolve, or any external removal), `unlink()` raises `FileNotFoundError`, which is not a `FindingError`.

## Evidence

Lines 569-581: `path = matches[0]` then `append_ledger(root, rec)` then `path.unlink()`. `main`'s handler (line 1108) only catches `FindingError`, so the `FileNotFoundError` escapes as an uncaught traceback with a nonzero exit. Two concurrent `resolve <id>` calls both pass `glob("*/open/<id>.md")` and both `append_ledger`, producing two identical ledger lines for one id; `validate_ledger_record` has no cross-line duplicate-id check, so the duplicate persists (only `resolved_records`'s last-wins read hides it).

## Impact

A crash-with-traceback instead of a clean error when the open file is already gone, plus a silently duplicated resolved record under concurrent resolves.

## Fix

Use `path.unlink(missing_ok=True)` and treat an already-removed file as an idempotent no-op; make the ledger append the durable commit. If concurrent same-store use is ever supported, guard ledger mutation with an advisory lock.

## Resolution
path.unlink(missing_ok=True) stops the uncaught FileNotFoundError crash when the open file is already gone. The lock-free double-append under genuinely simultaneous resolves remains a documented limitation of the single-agent store.
title: Concurrent or repeated resolve double-appends the ledger and crashes on an already-unlinked open file

body:
## Problem

`owner` and `repo` (raw argv, split on `/`) are string-interpolated into a REST URL and a `gh api` path with no allow-list and no `urllib.parse.quote`. Only `pr_number` is hardened via `int()`. Values containing `/`, `?`, `#`, or `..` are not rejected.

## Evidence

`base = f"https://api.github.com/repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}/comments"` (line 210) and `_gh_rest_paginate(f"repos/{owner}/{repo}/pulls/{pr_number}/comments")` (line 205). `fetch-pr-comments.py foo "bar/../../orgs/some-org/repos/other" 1` rewrites the request to a different endpoint via path traversal; a value with `?` injects query params onto the authenticated request. The GraphQL path (lines 141-189) is safe because it passes owner/repo as query variables. Additionally, `_token_rest_paginate` (lines 99-118) rebuilds the token-bearing `Request` for whatever URL the `rel="next"` Link header contains without asserting the host is still `api.github.com`, and `resp.read()` is unbounded.

## Impact

Path/query injection into an authenticated GitHub request when owner/repo come from an untrusted source (e.g. the `cr` flow deriving them from a git remote or PR context), redirecting the token-bearing request to an unintended endpoint.

## Fix

In `main()`, validate owner and repo against `^[A-Za-z0-9._-]+$` (GitHub's own charset) right after parsing and exit 2 on mismatch. Additionally assert `urllib.parse.urlsplit(next_url).netloc == "api.github.com"` before following a Link header, and optionally cap `resp.read(MAX_BYTES)`.

## Resolution
owner/repo validated against [A-Za-z0-9._-]+ in main() (exit 2 on bad); Link-header pagination now refuses to send the token to any host other than api.github.com. Tests added.
title: owner/repo interpolated into GitHub REST URL and gh api path without validation or encoding

body:
## Problem

Every PostToolUse hook repeats an identical `REPO_ROOT` resolution and an identical stdin -> file_path -> resolved-Path block. That is ~12 duplicated lines across five files, and the copies already differ inconsistently (three guard `if not file_path: return`, two do not).

## Evidence

In validate-skill-hook.py, validate-json-hook.py, check-version-sync-hook.py, ruff-hook.py, and validate-audit-findings-hook.py: `REPO_ROOT = Path(os.environ.get("CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR", os.environ.get("REPO_ROOT", _default)))`, then `tool_input = data.get("tool_input") or {}`, `file_path = tool_input.get("file_path") or data.get("file_path") or data.get("path") or ""`, and `path = (raw if raw.is_absolute() else REPO_ROOT / raw).resolve()` — verbatim across all five.

## Impact

A change to the hook-input contract must be applied in five places; the existing guard inconsistency shows the copies already drift.

## Fix

Add `scripts/hooks/_hooklib.py` with `repo_root()` and `edited_path(stdin_data) -> Path | None` (the pattern already exists — internal scripts import `scripts/common.py`). Each hook becomes `path = edited_path(json.load(sys.stdin))` plus an early return.

## Resolution
Added scripts/hooks/_hooklib.py (repo_root, edited_path); all six hooks now share it instead of copying the preamble. 23 hook tests pass.
title: Five PostToolUse hooks copy the same stdin-parse and path-resolve preamble verbatim

body:
## Problem

`read_toml_version` manually tracks `[project]` vs `[tool.*]` sections with two regexes to pull one version string, on a script that already declares `requires-python = ">=3.11"` where `tomllib` is stdlib.

## Evidence

Lines 29-42 are a 14-line `in_project` state machine (`re.match(r"^\[project\]\s*$", line)` ... `re.match(r"""^version\s*=\s*["']([^"']+)["']""", line)`) to extract `[project].version`.

## Impact

More surface to break on TOML edge cases (multiline strings, inline tables) than the stdlib parser it reimplements.

## Fix

Replace the body with `import tomllib; return tomllib.loads((REPO_ROOT / rel_path).read_text("utf-8"))["project"]["version"]`, raising `KeyError` on miss as today. Note: the `update_toml` writer in bump-version.py must stay regex-based (it must preserve formatting); only this read path is replaceable.

## Resolution
read_toml_version now uses stdlib tomllib; malformed TOML surfaces as KeyError to the existing handler. Tests for mismatch and malformed TOML added.
title: Hand-rolled section-scoped TOML scan reinvents stdlib tomllib

body:
## Problem

`check-version-sync.py` is the release gate that asserts one version across all five manifests. Three manifests route through the shared `CHECKS` loop whose mismatch branch is covered, but `pyproject.toml` is verified by a separate hand-written branch (lines 56-64) that no test ever drives with an out-of-sync value.

## Evidence

Every pyproject supplied in `tests/test_check_version_sync.py` keeps `[project]` version at the base `1.0.0`: `_make_repo` default (line 24), `test_tool_section_version_ignored` (line 74 — the `9.9.9` is under `[tool.poetry]`), and `test_single_quoted_pyproject_version_no_false_alarm` (line 81). The mismatch tests desync marketplace/plugin, never `[project]`. So lines 57-59 (`if toml_ver != base:` -> print MISMATCH + `fail = True`) run in zero tests.

## Impact

Mutating `if toml_ver != base:` to `if False:`, or dropping `fail = True`, passes the full green suite while `pyproject.toml` drifts out of sync — the gate stops catching drift for one of the five manifests and a release ships an inconsistent version undetected.

## Fix

Add a test mirroring `test_marketplace_mismatch_returns_1`: `_make_repo(tmp_path, pyproject='[project]\nname = "foo"\nversion = "9.9.9"\n')`, assert `_run(tmp_path) == 1`, and assert `"pyproject.toml"` and `"9.9.9"` appear in captured stdout.

## Resolution
Added test_pyproject_version_mismatch_returns_1 (and a malformed-TOML test) exercising the previously-untested pyproject branch of the version gate.
title: pyproject version-mismatch branch of the 5-manifest sync gate is untested

body:
## Problem

`check-rules-anatomy.py` is a working, shipped tool that flags hedged/vague/non-imperative rules, but nothing runs it. Only `validate-rules.py` (structure only: kebab name, `.md`, frontmatter, glob freshness) is bound to `make check`, pre-commit, and CI.

## Evidence

`grep -rn anatomy Makefile .pre-commit-config.yaml .github/workflows/` returns nothing. `make check` runs validate + validate-rules + version-sync + index-check + lint + format-check + test + pre-commit — no anatomy step. Bypass: add `.claude/rules/vague.md` full of "might/could/consider" hedging; `validate-rules.py` (structure-only) passes, pre-commit `validate-rules` passes, CI `Validate rules files` passes. The quality tool never executes.

## Impact

The repo ships a hostile rules auditor to consumers but never applies it to its own rules; vague/advisory rules can land on `main` ungated — the exact defect class this project exists to catch.

## Fix

Add a `rules-anatomy` target invoking `python3 skills/nitpicker/scripts/check-rules-anatomy.py` (fail on error_count>0), wire it into `make check`, a pre-commit `local` hook scoped to `^\.claude/rules/`, and a CI step. First resolve the one live match it reports (`skill-style.md` "might" is inside a quoted example of forbidden words — a false positive to suppress).

## Resolution
Wired check-rules-anatomy.py as a pre-commit local hook (rules-anatomy), so make check and CI (pre-commit --all-files) now gate rule quality on its High/Critical exit code.
title: Rule-quality checker check-rules-anatomy.py is wired into no gate — rule anatomy is ungated

body:
## Problem

`use-uv-runner.md` mandates a specific shebang and metadata per tier: shipped tools must begin `#!/usr/bin/env python3` with no `# /// script` block; internal tools must begin `#!/usr/bin/env -S uv run --quiet` with a `# /// script` block. The only gate, `check-stdlib-only.py`, parses the AST for non-stdlib imports and never inspects the shebang or the metadata block, and it only looks at `skills/*/scripts/`.

## Evidence

`find_violations` walks `ast.Import`/`ast.ImportFrom` nodes only (lines 52-56); no line reads the script's first line or scans for `# /// script`. It globs `skills/*/scripts/*.py` (lines 69, 89), so internal `scripts/` and `scripts/hooks/` get no shebang/metadata check at all. Bypass A: a shipped `skills/nitpicker/scripts/foo.py` with a `uv run` shebang + `# /// script` block but only stdlib imports passes the gate and ships, then breaks on a consumer that execs it without uv. Bypass B: an internal `scripts/foo.py` with `#!/usr/bin/env python3` and a third-party import trips no gate (stdlib-only skips it) and fails when run as `uv run` expects. All four shipped tools are currently correct, so this is a latent coverage hole.

## Impact

A shipped tool can carry the internal-tooling shebang/metadata undetected and fail on consumer machines (the "uv cannot be assumed to exist" failure the rule forbids); internal tooling can silently drift from the runner contract.

## Fix

In `check-stdlib-only.py` (or a small sibling), also assert each shipped script's first line is exactly `#!/usr/bin/env python3` with no `# /// script` marker, and extend a mirror check to `scripts/` and `scripts/hooks/` requiring the uv shebang + `# /// script` block.

## Resolution
check-stdlib-only.py now enforces the runner contract: shipped tools require the python3 shebang and no PEP-723 block; internal runnable scripts require the uv shebang. Shebang-less libraries exempt. Tests added.
title: Script-runner contract (shebang plus /// script block) is unenforced on both script tiers

body:
## Problem

The rule's SHA-pin and least-privilege clauses are enforced by zizmor (pre-commit + CI), but its final clause — every job must have a `name:` and every workflow a `concurrency:` group — is outside zizmor's check set (unpinned-uses, excessive-permissions, injection, dangerous-triggers). No other check covers it.

## Evidence

`.pre-commit-config.yaml` runs zizmor with `args: [--pedantic, --offline]` (line 76); zizmor has no rule asserting job-name or concurrency-group presence. Bypass: add a workflow job with no `name:` and no workflow-level `concurrency:` — zizmor passes, pre-commit and CI pass. Current workflows happen to comply.

## Impact

Two of the rule's four clauses are advisory-only; a future workflow can violate them silently.

## Fix

Add a small check asserting each job has a `name:` and each workflow a `concurrency:` group (a `local` pre-commit hook over `.github/workflows/*.yml` suffices), or downgrade the clause in the rule to acknowledge it is style-advisory.

## Resolution
github-actions-security.md now states explicitly that zizmor enforces SHA-pins/permissions/injection and the name/concurrency clause is review-verified, closing the silent-gap the finding named. A bespoke YAML checker was not built for already-compliant workflows.
title: github-actions-security job-name/concurrency clause is not enforced by zizmor

body:
## Problem

The `pull_request` trigger is `types: [opened, edited, synchronize]`. It omits `reopened`, so a PR closed and reopened without new commits fires no run; and the heavyweight `validate` job carries no `if:` scoping it away from `edited`, so it re-runs in full on every PR title/body edit.

## Evidence

`pull_request: types: [opened, edited, synchronize]` (lines 9-10). A reopened PR with no new commits triggers nothing — if `Validate` is a required check, the branch can merge on a stale/absent result (a gap in the authoritative gate `commit-gate-integrity.md` depends on). Separately, the `validate` job (checkout, uv install, full pytest, ruff, `pre-commit run --all-files`) has no `if:` guard, so a metadata-only title edit re-runs the entire suite. The `edited` trigger is only needed by the `pr-title` lint job.

## Impact

A required check can go stale on reopen; CI minutes are wasted re-running the full suite on every title typo-fix.

## Fix

Add `reopened` to the `types` list, and gate the `validate` job with `if: github.event_name != 'pull_request' || github.event.action != 'edited'` (the `pr-title` job still runs on `edited`; `synchronize` still covers new commits).

## Resolution
Added reopened to the pull_request types and gated the heavyweight validate job off the edited action (title/body edits no longer rerun the full suite; synchronize still covers new commits).
title: pull_request trigger omits reopened and reruns the full validate suite on every title/body edit

body:
## Problem

CLAUDE.md's Versioning table and `.github/copilot-instructions.md`'s Commit Message Convention both define `feat:` with the parenthetical "(new command)", implying `feat:` is only for adding a nitpicker command. Taken literally this would mislabel legitimate feature commits as `fix:`.

## Evidence

CLAUDE.md: "| `feat:` | Minor bump (new command) |"; copilot-instructions.md carries the same row. Contradicting reality on this branch: `190ab12 feat: add findings.py CLI and per-finding audit store` (a shipped tool, not a command), `9c7a17e feat: drive the default audit through an external coverage checklist` (adds a shared ref, no command), `df5fc3c feat: bind step-as-task-list ... in conventions` (edits `_conventions.md`, no command). AGENTS.md line 30 gets it right ("`feat:` minor" with no gloss), so the three docs disagree.

## Impact

A contributor following the doc literally would downgrade real feature commits to `fix:`, under-versioning releases (patch instead of minor).

## Fix

Drop the "(new command)" parenthetical in CLAUDE.md and copilot-instructions.md; match AGENTS.md — `feat:` = any new user-facing capability (new command or shipped-tool/behavior feature).

## Resolution
feat: gloss changed to 'new command or feature' in CLAUDE.md and copilot-instructions.md, aligning with AGENTS.md and the repo's own feat: commits.
title: feat: glossed as (new command) contradicts the repo's own feat: commits and AGENTS.md

body:
## Problem

All six dev hooks read `os.environ.get("CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR", os.environ.get("REPO_ROOT", _default))`. `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` is a Claude Code standard, but the custom `REPO_ROOT` fallback is documented in no config surface.

## Evidence

e.g. `validate-audit-findings-hook.py:20`. `grep -rln REPO_ROOT CLAUDE.md AGENTS.md README.md` finds nothing (only the generic `agent-hooks.md` command doc mentions the name). A contributor running hooks outside Claude Code has no documented knob and no `.env.example`; setting the wrong `REPO_ROOT` silently points validation at the wrong tree.

## Impact

Minor — undocumented internal plumbing; falls back to a computed path, so no default-safety issue.

## Fix

Add one line to CLAUDE.md's Configuration section noting the `CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR` -> `REPO_ROOT` -> computed-parent resolution order, or drop the `REPO_ROOT` fallback if unused.

## Resolution
Documented the CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR -> REPO_ROOT -> computed-parent resolution order in CLAUDE.md's Configuration section.
title: REPO_ROOT env override read by every hook is documented nowhere

body:
## Problem

`package.json` sets `"type": "module"` but the repo contains no `.js`/`.mjs`/`.ts` files and declares no `main`/`bin`/`exports`/`files` and no dependencies. The field is inert and misleading.

## Evidence

The tracked-file census shows no JavaScript/TypeScript sources; package.json is only metadata consumed by the `skills` installer, which reads name/version/description and does not need `type`.

## Impact

Cosmetic drift — implies an ESM JS package that does not exist.

## Fix

Drop `"type": "module"` (keep name/version/description, which the `skills` installer does read).

## Resolution
Removed the inert "type": "module" from package.json (no JS/TS in the repo).
title: package.json declares type: module with zero JS/TS in the repo

body:
## Problem

`use-context-mode.md` requires routing all inspection through context-mode and permits `# ctx-ok` only for genuine mutations or tiny fixed output. Enforcement comes entirely from the external context-mode plugin's PreToolUse hook; nothing committed to this repo enforces it, and nothing validates that `# ctx-ok` is appended only to mutations.

## Evidence

`.claude/settings.json` contains no PreToolUse entry — the routing block comes from the installed `mcp__plugin_context-mode` plugin, not repo config. In any environment where that plugin is absent (a fresh clone, CI, a different agent) every inspection command runs as plain Bash with no gate; and even with the plugin, appending `# ctx-ok` to a read-only command is accepted unchecked.

## Impact

The rule is only as strong as an out-of-tree plugin the repo cannot guarantee is installed, and the `# ctx-ok` carve-out is unauditable. This is inherent to a runtime-behavior rule with no committed-artifact signal.

## Fix

Note in the rule that enforcement depends on the context-mode plugin being installed and treat it as best-effort where the plugin is absent; there is no committed artifact to lint.

## Resolution
use-context-mode.md now states enforcement is best-effort and depends on the context-mode plugin's PreToolUse hook being installed.
title: context-mode rule and its # ctx-ok escape hatch have no in-repo enforcement

body:
## Problem

`75e629d feat: validate JSON/YAML config against schemas in pre-commit and CI` touches only development tooling — no shipped skill instruction, tool, or command changes. Under the repo's own convention table it is `chore:`/`ci:` (no bump), not `feat:` (minor bump).

## Evidence

The commit's entire diff is dev/build surface: `.pre-commit-config.yaml`, both `.github/workflows/*.yml`, `.yamllint.yaml`, `.prettierignore`, `.release-please-config.json`, new `schemas/*.schema.json`, a one-line `$schema` key on `plugin.json`/`marketplace.json`, and generated findings-store files. No file under `skills/nitpicker/` (the shipped skill) changes.

## Impact

release-please counts this as a feature and adds a minor bump. On this branch it is masked by the `feat!:` major (dca9a7d -> 2.0.0), but as commit discipline it is type-overstatement; on a non-major branch it would silently inflate the release.

## Fix

Should have been `ci:` or `chore:`. Not rewritable now without history surgery; keep CI/tooling-only changes off `feat:` going forward.

## Resolution
Branch history rebuilt: the tooling/schema change (formerly 75e629d feat:) now lands under a chore: commit, so no spurious minor bump exists in the history.
title: CI/tooling-only change committed as feat: — spurious minor bump masked by the 2.0 major

body:
## Problem

The `owner`/`repo` validation comment claims the charset regex `_OWNER_REPO_RE` "blocks /, .., ?, @", but `..` is composed only of `.` characters, which are inside the allowed set `[A-Za-z0-9._-]`, so `..` passes validation. The stated guarantee is false.

## Evidence

`_OWNER_REPO_RE = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z0-9._-]+")` (line 38). Verified: `re.compile(r"[A-Za-z0-9._-]+").fullmatch("..")` returns a match. Comment at lines 242-243: "reject anything outside GitHub's allowed name charset (blocks /, .., ?, @, ...)". `/`, `?`, `@` are genuinely blocked (not in the charset); `..` is not.

## Impact

No live exploit today: both sinks pin the host — `_token_rest_paginate` builds `https://api.github.com/...` and never follows an off-host `next` link (line 122), and `_gh_rest_paginate`/GraphQL go through `gh api`. So `owner=".."` only yields a 404 on a fixed host. The defect is the misleading guarantee: a maintainer who later reuses this validator for a configurable/Enterprise host, trusting the comment that `..` is rejected, inherits a path-traversal hole on the API path that the code never actually prevented.

## Fix

Either make the comment true by rejecting the two traversal tokens explicitly (after the charset check: `if value in ('.', '..'): reject`), or correct the comment to state that `.`/`..` are permitted and traversal safety rests on the pinned host. The explicit reject is the smaller, safer diff and matches the comment's intent.

## Resolution
Reject bare '.'/'..' explicitly in owner/repo validation (fetch-pr-comments.py:244) and corrected the comment; added regression params to test_malicious_owner_repo_exits_2.
title: owner/repo comment claims it blocks '..' but the regex permits it

body:
## Problem

`check-rules-anatomy.py` (shipped skill tool) and `validate-rules.py` (internal) both validate the same `.claude/rules/*.md` files with the same rule set, each carrying its own near-identical copy of a multi-line frontmatter parser and a symlink-safe recursive directory walker.

## Evidence

`parse_rules_frontmatter` (validate-rules.py:13-64) and `_parse_frontmatter` (check-rules-anatomy.py:34-80) are ~50-line twins; `_iter_rules_dir` (validate-rules.py:138-161) and `_iter_rules` (check-rules-anatomy.py:159-183) are ~25-line twins. They have already drifted: check-rules-anatomy parses YAML flow-style `paths: ["a","b"]` (lines 71-74) while validate-rules rejects it (line 113) — same input, opposite verdicts.

## Impact

Every rule-format change must be made twice or the two validators disagree; a flow-style `paths:` line passes the shipped tool but fails the internal CI gate. The drift is not hypothetical — it already exists.

## Fix

Make one source of truth: delete the parser/walker/per-check logic from `validate-rules.py` and have it import the shipped `check-rules-anatomy.py` helpers (internal tooling depending on the stdlib-only shipped tool points the dependency the safe direction), then render the returned findings as text + exit code.

## Resolution
Deduplicated: check-rules-anatomy.py (shipped, stdlib-only) is now the single source of truth for the frontmatter parser and rules walker; validate-rules.py imports them via importlib (safe internal->shipped direction). Unified parser on the YAML-correct keep-on-blank-line behavior; ~70 lines of twin logic removed. 393 tests pass.
